rulings.Case Title: DM 2465 VAN NESS HOLDINGS MEMEBER INVESTOR LLC VS. NAVID SARRAFZADEH
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Of Stay Pursuant To Ccp Section 916 Or In The Alternative Motion To Waive, Reduce, Or Modify Undertaking Amount Pursuant To Ccp Section 995.240; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Ali A. Aalaei
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the DISCOVERY/LAW & MOTION CALENDAR for Friday, May-09-2025, Line 1. RESPONDENT NAVID SARRAFZADEH'S Motion For Order Of Stay Pursuant To Ccp Section 916 Or In The Alternative Motion To Waive, Reduce, Or Modify Undertaking Amount Pursuant To Ccp Section 995.240. Respondent and judgment debtor Navid Sarrafzadeh's motion for stay of proceedings pursuant to CCP 916 or in the alternative to reduce or waive the previously ordered undertaking amount is denied. ***THE COURT'S FULL AND COMPLETE TENTATIVE RULING HAS BEEN EMAILED TO COUNSEL*** For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

Matter on the DISCOVERY/LAW & MOTION CALENDAR for Friday, May-09-2025, Line 2. PETITIONER RAMONA MAYON For Writ Of Mandate. The motion for writ of mandate is denied without prejudice. To obtain a writ of mandate, petitioner must serve the opposing party (here, the City and County of San Francisco) with the underlying petition, which does not yet appear to have occurred here, and must serve the opposing party with its motion for writ of mandate. Service of a motion must be made at least sixteen court days before the hearing, with additional time if the moving party uses a method of service other than personal service. Petitioner provides no proof of service to show that she has complied with these requirements. To have the power to order relief, a court must have jurisdiction over the parties, and the court has no jurisdiction where one party has not been served in the manner required by law. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) rulings.Case Title: WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. VS. JUGENA NORRIS, AN INDIVIDUAL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Pursuant To Ccp437c
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the DISCOVERY/LAW & MOTION CALENDAR for Friday, May-09-2025, Line 3. PLAINTIFF WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Pursuant To Ccp437c. The unopposed motion for summary judgment is denied as untimely served. Wells Fargo's proof of service shows that service was made by mail within California on February 19, 2025, 79 days before the hearing. CCP 437c(a)(2) provides, "Notice of the motion and supporting papers shall be served on all other parties to the action at least 81 days before the time appointed for hearing. If the notice is served by mail, the required 81-day period of notice shall be increased by 5 days if the place of address is within the State of California . . ." For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. rulings.Case Title: JANE DOE ET AL VS. SAN FRANCISCO CONSERVATORY OF MUSIC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT AXEL STRAUSS DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT (ADDED FOR POSTING OF TENTATIVE RULING)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the DISCOVERY/LAW & MOTION CALENDAR for Friday, May-09-2025, Line 4. DEFENDANT AXEL STRAUSS DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT (ADDED FOR POSTING OF TENTATIVE RULING). ***PART TWO OF TWO*** In addition, subdivision (e) itself and subdivision (e)(2) are both broadly worded. Subdivision (e) says it revives "any claim seeking to recover damages" as a result of sexual assault, a broad phrasing. Subdivision (e)(2) says the revival provisions reach "claims brought by a plaintiff" meeting specified criteria. The revival provisions are not expressly limited by the defendants against whom the claims are brought (i.e. only entities, as Strauss would have it), and thus the court declines to read a defendant-specific limitation into the statute. The parties argue about the significance of Jane Doe #21 (S.H.) v. CFR Enterprises Inc. (2023) 93 Cal.App.5th 1199, to the interpretation of Subdivision (e). Jane Doe #21 does not directly answer the question presented here, but it does provide guidance that courts in interpreting Sec. 340.16 should take care not to add limiting language to the statute. (Id. at p. 1212.) That guidance supports the court's interpretation here. The court concludes that the plain text of Sec. 340.16(e) revives Doe's claims against Strauss as well as against the Conservatory. No resort to legislative history is needed. (See, e.g., People v. Johnson (2002) 28 Cal.4th 240, 244 ["Because the statutory language is generally the most reliable indicator of that intent, we look first at the words themselves, giving them their usual and ordinary meaning and construing them in context. If the plain language of the statute is clear and unambiguous, our inquiry ends, and we need not embark on judicial construction."] [internal citations omitted].) To the extent the court considers legislative history, it finds that the Legislature's ameliorative purposes in enacting Subdivision (e) support Doe's interpretation. (See Doe's RJN, Ex. 1.) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. rulings.Case Title: JANE DOE ET AL VS. SAN FRANCISCO CONSERVATORY OF MUSIC ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the DISCOVERY/LAW & MOTION CALENDAR for Friday, May-09-2025, Line 4. DEFENDANT AXEL STRAUSS' DEMURRER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT. ***PART ONE OF TWO*** Defendant Axel Strauss demurs to Jane Doe's causes of action against him (the First, Second, and Sixth Causes of Action) as time-barred. For the reasons stated herein, the court overrules the demurrer. The court grants both parties' unopposed requests for judicial notice. Doe's Second Amended Complaint (SAC) alleges that defendant San Francisco Conservatory of Music (Conservatory) was aware of reports of sexual assault and harassment by faculty and others against students but deliberately failed to investigate or take action. (SAC paras. 17-22.) Doe, a then-student, alleges that Strauss was employed as faculty by the Conservatory and sexually assaulted her in 2007. (SAC paras. 33-35.) After Doe confronted Strauss, her grade dropped, and she left the school. (SAC paras. 37, 40.) Doe filed this action on December 28, 2023. Strauss contends that Doe's causes of action against him are time-barred. The limitations period for civil claims of sexual assault against an adult victim is generally 10 years from the date of the last assault or attempted assault. (CCP Sec. 340.16(a)(1).) The viability of Doe's claims against Strauss turn on whether they fall within a revival statute, CCP Sec. 340.16(e) ["Subdivision (e)"]. Subdivision (e) revives "any claim seeking to recover damages suffered as a result of sexual assault" as specified within the subdivision. (Id. subdivision (e).) Subdivision (e)(2) specifies what is revived: "claims brought by a plaintiff who alleges all of the following: (A) The plaintiff was sexually assaulted. (B) One or more entities are legally responsible for damages arising out of the sexual assault. (C) The entity or entities . . . engaged in a cover up or attempted a cover up of a previous instance or allegations of sexual assault by an alleged perpetrator of such abuse." Strauss argues that Subdivision (e) revives only claims against an entity who engaged in a cover-up and not claims against the individual who allegedly perpetrated the assault. Doe argues that Subdivision (e) revives claims against the individual perpetrator as well as the entity with respondeat superior or other derivative liability for the perpetrator's conduct. The court concludes that Doe has the better reading of the statute. Subdivision (e)(5) expressly instructs that "[t]his subdivision revives any related claims, including but not limited to, wrongful termination and sexual harassment, arising out of the sexual assault that is the basis for a claim pursuant to the subdivision." Doe's claims against Strauss for sexual assault are related to her claims against the Conservatory for sexual assault because the claims arise out of the same facts. The plain language of the statute indicates the inclusion of these claims in the revival. ***END OF PART ONE OF TWO*** =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: BANK OF AMERICA, N.A. VS. LUIS M ARREOLA GARCIA
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the DISCOVERY/LAW & MOTION CALENDAR for Friday, May-09-2025, Line 5. PLAINTIFF BANK OF AMERICA, N.A.'S MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS. Plaintiff Bank of America N.A.'s motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted. The court may decide a motion for judgment on the pleadings on the face of the complaint and matters judicially noticeable, including judicial admissions. (Pang v. Beverly Hosp., Inc. (2000) 79 Cal.App.4th 986, 989-990.) The court grants the request for judicial notice. In the complaint, Plaintiff pleaded an open book account for money due claim in the amount of $6,229.53. Defendant has admitted a balance owed of greater than the amount pleaded. (Ex. 1 to Langedyk Dec.) The court accordingly grants the motion. Plaintiff may submit a proposed judgment for the amount pleaded of $6,229.53 plus the amount in its cost bill of $418.61.
